body:
Yves,

Forty people by next Saturday? I would say you're insane but honestly that's a Tuesday for you. Send me the client brief when you have it — I can help you cross-check quantities before you submit the charcuterie order. Wednesday is still doable if you move now, not tomorrow-now.

I'll text Henri tonight so he's expecting your call Sunday. He'll act surprised, pretend he wasn't sitting by the phone since Friday. Classique.

And stop with the "he seemed fine, don't read into it." I wasn't reading into anything. Now I am. What did he actually say?

— Nat

Nat,

Coffee ran long this morning — Papa Luc wanted to sit an extra half-hour just talking about nothing, which honestly I didn't mind. Pulled up the catering inbox on my phone right there at the table. Three new client inquiries since Thursday and one of them wants a full grazing spread for forty people by next Saturday. Crisse, the timeline on that one is going to be tight.

Calendar's showing I'm on afternoon shift Thursday and Friday, so mise en place starts mid-morning both days. I've already flagged the ingredient list — need to confirm the charcuterie order before Wednesday or we're scrambling. If you talk to the Montréal side before I do, tell Henri I'll call him Sunday.

Papa seemed okay. Ate well, we laughed, it was fine. Don't read into it.

— Yves

Nat,

The 15th just came through — got the scheduling email ten minutes ago. Both days confirmed, Jess is briefed, I'm set.

I hear you on the lead time. It's on my list, right between finalizing the catering mise en place for Thursday and convincing Sienna that I don't actually live at that hotel. I'll do better.

And dinner — deal. I'm thinking something with at least five ingredients, just for you.

T'es la meilleure, comme toujours.
Yves

Nat,

Still no confirmation from scheduling on my morning-shift leave request for the 14th and 15th — I go on the floor in three days and nothing's been cleared. Jess already said she can cover the desk for one of those, but I need the official approval on file before I can hand anything off.

Can you nudge whoever's sitting on it? I'd call directly but you know how that goes — I'll spend my entire coffee with Papa on hold, and then I'm showing up to The Hawthorne Grand half-awake and twice as grumpy.

Merci d'avance,
Yves

Yves,

Looked into it this morning. The 14th is approved — that's done. The 15th is still waiting on one more sign-off from your floor supervisor, but scheduling says it should clear by end of day tomorrow. I'll forward the confirmation the moment it hits my inbox.

And yes, I'm saying it: submit these at least a week out. I'm a support agent, not a miracle worker, and 72-hour turnarounds on leave requests make everyone look bad. You know the process.

You owe me a proper dinner. Something with more than four ingredients.

— Nat

Nat,

11:00 works. Lock it. And yeah, overnight dry rub is fine — I've done it that way before and honestly the crust is better for it anyway.

One more thing: the catering inbox had a Friday inquiry come in while I was on shift — couple wanting a private dinner for eight, July 12th. I flagged it but didn't respond yet. Tabarnak, the timing on that one's going to step on my Sunday wind-down planning pass if we're not careful. Can you triage it before Thursday so I'm not walking into the weekend blind?

Sienna's already warned me I'm distracted this week. She's not wrong.

Yves

Nat,

The Thursday window isn't working. Hawthorne has me on afternoon shift both Thu and Fri this week, and I can't get mise en place started before 4:30 at the earliest — that's forty minutes behind where I need to be for the Delacroix booking Saturday. I told the client we'd have the cold apps staged and the protein brined by end of Friday. Right now that's not happening.

I need you to either push the Saturday call time back by an hour or get someone on-site Thursday morning to start the dry components without me. Jess offered but she's already covering the front desk until noon. Options are thin.

Tell me what's moveable.

Yves

Yves,

Okay, bad timing all around — I hear you. I already reached out to the Delacroix contact this morning and floated a soft push on the Saturday start. They came back flexible; they'll take an 11:00 a.m. instead of 10:00. That buys you the hour you need without changing anything downstream.

For Thursday, skip the brine start — I'll send you a revised prep order tonight that front-loads Friday. The dry rub on the protein holds just as well overnight if you're starting no later than 5:00. Not ideal but it's clean.

Confirm you're good with the 11:00 Saturday call and I'll lock it in with the client.

Nat

Yves,

Saturday is locked — Delacroix confirmed 11:00, I sent the updated run-of-show to your catering folder.

July 12th inquiry is triaged. Eight-person private dinner, dietary flags are noted (one vegetarian, one shellfish allergy), and the client is warm — they found you through the Pemberton event last spring. I drafted a reply holding the date tentatively and asking for their menu preferences. It's in drafts waiting on your sign-off, nothing urgent before Thursday.

Tell Sienna she's right and take the Sunday planning pass seriously this week. Your mise en place rhythm only works when you actually protect the reset time. I'm not covering for a burnt-out version of you.

Nat

Yves,

Two hours late on checkout and you held the smile — you are a better person than me, full stop, no contest. I would've started speaking exclusively in French by hour one and you know exactly what kind of French I mean.

The playlist pipeline is genuinely elite and I refuse to admit I've been quietly building my own version since March. The Armagnac I endorse completely. The "no screens after one" part I do not believe for a single second — you're reading catering emails at two in the morning and we both know it.

Eat something green occasionally. Sleep more than five hours. I will call Sunday; have the garlic pasta recipe ready because I'm writing it down this time.

— Nat

P.S. The kompas block is perfect and you were right and I will never say that again out loud.

Nat,

Survived the shift. Barely. Late checkout on the Pemberton party — two hours, zero apology — and I held the smile the entire time, so you owe me something. Got home around eleven-fifteen, threw on the playlist, started the wind-down before I even changed out of the uniform.

Gainsbourg into Khruangbin, then the old Haitian kompas block you made fun of at Christmas. Cast-iron on the stove, roasted garlic and whatever was left in the crisper, poured two fingers of that Armagnac Papa brought up last visit. By midnight I felt close enough to human to sleep.

That's the whole system. Playlist, something on the stove, one drink, no screens after one. Works until it doesn't. Tonight it worked.

Miss you. Call me this weekend.

— Y

Yves,

Found it — scope mismatch. When you re-authed last week, the gmail.send scope didn't make it into the consent screen. The token you're holding is valid but missing that permission entirely, which is why the 403 is consistent.

Fix is straightforward: revoke the current token from your Google account security page (direct link: myaccount.google.com/permissions), then re-run the auth flow using the updated config file I'm attaching to this email — not the Thursday one, that one had the bug. Wait about ten minutes after revocation before you re-auth, Google's cache clears slow.

Your catering inbox data is untouched, it's only the credential layer we're replacing. Should take you fifteen minutes tops.

Ping me after if anything looks off. You're close.

— Nat

Hey Nat,

Still broken — wanted to loop back before the week got away from me. Tried the re-auth flow you sent Thursday, same 403 on messages.send, same stack trace. I'm pasting it at the bottom. I didn't want to nuke the OAuth credential and start fresh without checking with you first, especially since the catering inbox is actually getting traffic now.

The whole point was to pull booking confirmations out automatically and log them into the sheet so I'm not doing it by hand between desk shifts at the Hawthorne. Right now it's manual and it's eating into the only twenty quiet minutes I get before the catering inbox starts firing.

On shift until 4 today. Evenings are better if you want to screen-share.

— Yves

[stack trace below]

Nat,

Tabarnac, it worked.

Revoked at 5:47, waited the ten minutes, ran your config. Confirmations are pulling through — tested against three bookings from last week and all three logged clean into the sheet. First time this whole thing has actually done what I built it to do. Feels suspicious, honestly.

Merci, sérieusement. You saved me probably forty minutes a day of copy-paste misery. I owe you a proper dinner — you and Mathieu, you name the date and I'll build the menu around whatever you're both craving. No arguing, just show up.

À plus,
Yves

Nat,

Still broken. The Google Calendar API integration I asked about last week isn't syncing my catering blocks to the Hawthorne Grand calendar — Thursday and Friday mise en place windows keep getting wiped whenever the hotel pushes a bulk update. I've got a client confirmed for Friday afternoon and the slot just disappears on my end. Not great.

I set up the OAuth flow myself using the service account you sent over, but I'm getting a 403 on write requests even though I added the calendar scope. Read works fine. Write does not. Classic.

Can you check whether the service account actually has editor access on the shared calendar, or if there's a domain-wide delegation thing I missed? I'd rather know the actual problem than get a ticket number.

— Yves

Yves,

Okay, found it. The service account has viewer permissions on the shared Hawthorne Grand calendar — someone set it up read-only at the resource level, which overrides the scope you declared in the OAuth config. That's your 403.

To fix it: whoever manages the hotel's Google Workspace admin console needs to grant the service account editor access directly on that specific calendar (not just domain-wide). If you don't have admin access there, you'll need to loop in IT at the hotel. I can draft the request email if that's easier.

Also — are your catering blocks being created as separate events or are you trying to write into an existing hotel event? That changes the approach a bit. Let me know and I'll send you the corrected API call.

— Nat

Nat,

Merci. That tracks — I had a feeling it was a permissions thing and not my code. I don't have admin access to the Workspace console, Jess might though, she's been dealing with the hotel IT side more than me lately. I'll ask her tomorrow morning before the shift.

To answer your question: separate events, my own catering calendar, I just need them visible on the shared hotel view so the front desk isn't double-booking the prep kitchen. So I'm writing to my calendar and trying to share visibility across. Does that change what I need?

Also — si t'as deux minutes — can you confirm the token refresh logic doesn't need to change once the permissions are fixed? I'm refreshing every 55 minutes and I don't want to rebuild that whole piece if it's fine.

— Yves

Yves,

Yes, that changes things slightly — and actually makes it simpler. You don't need editor access on the hotel's calendar at all. Just share your catering calendar with the hotel's shared calendar address (view-only is fine for front desk), and your writes stay entirely within your own calendar where your service account already has full access. No IT escalation needed.

Your 55-minute refresh is fine. Access tokens expire at 60 minutes, so you're safely inside the window. Don't touch that logic.

Step-by-step: (1) In Google Calendar settings, share your catering calendar with the hotel's calendar ID — ask Jess for that address. (2) Set it to "see all event details." (3) Test a write to your calendar and confirm it shows on the hotel view. Should be a 10-minute fix once you have the ID.

Let me know if it doesn't show up on their end — there's one more setting that can block cross-calendar visibility in Workspace.

— Nat

Nat,

Still waiting on the webhook config you said you'd send Thursday. The catering clients are blowing up my personal number because the business line isn't routing notifications properly — had three missed confirms on Friday and almost double-booked a mise en place window because of it.

The API docs you linked last week reference a v16 endpoint but our dashboard is clearly running something older. Can you confirm which version we're actually provisioned on? I need to know before I rebuild the message templates.

Also — and I know this is a separate ticket technically — the opt-in flow is broken on mobile. Sienna tested it on her phone and the confirmation bubble never fires. Jess tried it too, same result. So it's not isolated.

Just need the webhook payload and the version number. That unblocks everything else. Merci d'avance.

— Yves

Yves,

Sorry for the delay — genuinely slipped through after the Thursday queue spike. Attaching the webhook config now, and yes, you're on v15.0 of the Cloud API. The v16 docs I sent were wrong, my fault entirely. I'll flag our internal wiki to stop surfacing that page.

On the opt-in flow bug: we have an open incident for exactly this. It's affecting accounts where the phone number was migrated before March. Fix is staged for Tuesday's deploy. No action needed on your end — the templates you've already built will work once it rolls out.

The double-booking near-miss sounds stressful, especially on a Friday shift. Once the webhook is live you should get real-time delivery receipts and that whole problem goes away. If anything in the config file looks off, reply here and I'll jump on a call same day.

You're all set to rebuild. Let me know how it lands.

— Nathalie

Nat,

Test user workaround worked — je t'aime, sérieusement. Story post went through on my personal account inside twenty minutes. Redirect URI was the culprit, exactly like you said, missing the trailing slash. Fixed it, regenerated the token, and now the scheduler is queuing correctly.

Still waiting on the full review to go live but at least I can show Jess an actual working demo before the shift tomorrow morning. She was starting to give me that look.

Thursday menu: smoked salmon blinis, whipped ricotta crostini with roasted cherry tomatoes, and a fig-and-prosciutto board. Papa's coming by for coffee beforehand so I'm testing the blinis on him first. Don't tell him that part.

Merci encore — you saved the week.
Yves

Yves,

Okay, first — yes, you need `instagram_content_publish`, you're right about that. The good news is you can test in sandbox mode right now without waiting for the review to clear. Add your personal IG account as a sandbox tester in the app dashboard under Roles → Testers, then any API calls you make will go through on that account only. Not ideal but it'll let you validate the flow before Thursday.

The bad news: Meta's review queue is genuinely backed up right now — I've seen 8–10 day waits on new permission requests all month. If you submitted six days ago you might actually be close. Keep checking the email associated with the developer account, they don't always ping the dashboard.

Also, double-check your redirect URI is HTTPS. I've seen that silently block the token exchange on a few setups. Send me a screenshot of the error if it's still throwing after you add the tester and I'll dig in.

Nat
P.S. What's the catering menu Thursday? Stop gatekeeping.

Nat,

Still no resolution on the Instagram API token — it's been six days since I submitted the app review request and the dashboard is just sitting there saying "in review." I need this working before Thursday's catering pop-up because the automated story posts are the whole point of the promo push.

I followed the setup steps you sent last week, added the business account correctly, and the webhook validation passed fine. The issue is the token scope — looks like `instagram_basic` alone isn't enough to post. I probably need `instagram_content_publish` added to the permissions, but Meta won't let me test it until the review clears.

Can you check if there's an expedite option or a workaround for sandbox posting in the meantime? Jess keeps asking me for the schedule and I have nothing to give her yet.

Merci,
Yves

Nat —

Coffee with Papa this morning ran long. He's in one of his moods — talking slow, wanted a second cup, kept circling back to the old Montréal apartment. I didn't rush him. But it pushed my whole morning back and I hit the catering inbox late.

Already three clients with change requests for next week. The Vandermeer event bumped their headcount from 14 to 22 with zero notice. That changes my mise en place timeline completely — I need to recalculate everything before Thursday. Did you see the Kellerman inquiry come through? No budget listed, of course.

Not panicking, just flagging. Tell me you checked the shared calendar already.

— Yv

Yves,

Revised list received — ingredient order is in. Vendor confirmed Wednesday delivery by 4pm, so you're clear.

Kellerman came back: budget is there, event is July 12th. I'm flagging it on the shared calendar and sending you the full brief. Manageable add if Thursday goes smoothly.

Sunday dinner is a yes from me. I'll check with Émile — you know how he is, needs a week's notice minimum or he suddenly 'forgot.' I'll handle Papa's invite too, give it the right framing so he shows up excited and not like he's been summoned.

Get some rest tonight. You sound tired even in writing.

— Nat

Yves,

Yes, I saw the Kellerman email — replied and asked for their budget range and event date. Haven't heard back yet but I gave them until EOD tomorrow. Don't hold the date for them yet.

Vandermeer at 22 is manageable but you're right that Thursday is tight. Can you push mise en place start to Wednesday evening instead? I can shift the ingredient order deadline if you send me a revised list by tonight. I'll handle the vendor call.

Also — Papa called me after your coffee. He sounded okay, just chatty. Think he needed the company more than the coffee today. You did good staying.

Nat

Nat —

Wednesday evening works. I'll have the revised list to you by 9pm. Swapping out two of the sauces anyway — sourcing issue on the shallots this week, Jess flagged it yesterday at the hotel. She's got good eyes, that one.

Hawthorne shift today was fine. Back-to-back check-ins until 2, then a group checkout that took forever, but nothing that wrecked me. Home by 7, cooking playlist already queued.

Ouais, je sais, pour Papa. Mon vieux, lui — il cherche quelque chose qu'on peut pas lui donner au téléphone. Ça fait mal de même. We should do a real Sunday dinner soon, all of us. Émile too, if he can pull himself away from whatever he's got going on.

Talk tomorrow.
— Yv

Nat,

Still waiting on the updated morning-shift departure window from scheduling — they said Tuesday, it's now Sunday. Can you ping Housekeeping Ops on my behalf? I'd do it myself but I'm already juggling the catering inbox before I leave and mornings are not the time to chase paperwork.

The issue is simple: my leave window on morning-shift days keeps getting logged as 07:45 but I'm consistently out the door by 07:20 after coffee with Papa. If that 25-minute gap keeps showing up wrong in the system, it flags me late on the shift board and Jess has to manually clear it every single time. She's been great about it but it's not her job.

Just need the record corrected to 07:20 effective departure. That's it. No form, no meeting.

Merci d'avance — t'es la meilleure,
Yves

Yves,

Got it. Already sent the correction request to Housekeeping Ops with the 07:20 departure time — flagged it as an ongoing discrepancy so they update the master record, not just the current week. You should see it reflected in the shift board by Tuesday morning at the latest. I copied Jess so she knows it's being handled and doesn't keep eating the manual fix.

For what it's worth, you should've sent this two weeks ago instead of letting it pile up. I know you hate admin before the first coffee. But next time it's faster if you just forward me the shift-board screenshot — I can usually sort it same day.

Let me know if they push back on anything. They won't, but let me know.

Nat

Nat,

Ingredient list for Thursday's mise en place came back wrong — three items flagged missing from the Delacroix party order and I didn't catch it until this morning. Need you to pull the catering inbox records from last week and tell me if they updated their brief after we locked confirmation.

Friday window is tighter. I'm doing a full shift at the Hawthorne before I even get to prep, so if anything else is off I want to know now, not at 3 p.m. when I'm already mid-julienne. Jess is on front desk Thursday morning and can't help me cross-check — you're the only one with the original file trail.

— Yves

Yves,

Found it. The Delacroix brief was updated on the 24th — they swapped the lemon herb salmon for a vegetarian centerpiece and added a nut allergy flag. Inbox timestamp reads 11:47 p.m., which is why it didn't surface in your morning review cycle.

I've corrected the ingredient list and pushed the updated version to your catering folder. Friday's Morrow event looks clean — no changes logged post-confirmation, so that window should hold.

You're fine going into both days. That said, the Sunday planning pass exists for exactly this reason. Use it.

— Nat

Nat,

Good. Thank you — that nut allergy flag could've been a whole incident, so I mean it.

Friday I'm starting mise en place at 2 after my Hawthorne shift closes, running on four hours of sleep and whatever Sienna left in the coffee pot. Calice, I need to build a real buffer into Thursdays going forward. Any chance you can flag me when the weekly confirmation window closes so I'm not chasing midnight updates myself?

Also — next week's client list, is it in the inbox yet? Want to do the full planning pass Sunday and actually be ahead for once.

Merci, Nat. T'es la meilleure.

— Yv

Nat,

Crashed harder than usual last night — back-to-back doubles at the Hawthorne this week and my body is collecting the bill. Finally nailed the wind-down though: shower first, then whatever's in the fridge gets turned into something worth eating. Had the cooking playlist going (Gainsbourg into Coltrane, don't ask, it works), poured two fingers of Amaro Averna, and just... breathed. Sienna says I look less like a ghost now, which I'll take.

You asked if I actually decompress or just swap one kind of busy for another. Honest answer: the cooking is the switch. Knife work especially. Everything else can wait.

Talk soon,
Yves

Yves,

Gainsbourg into Coltrane is the most "you" thing I've heard since you described your coffee order as "structural." Glad the routine is sticking. Though I have to ask — Amaro Averna on a weeknight after a double? You're not 24 anymore, mon frère.

Sienna texted me separately, by the way. She says the cooking thing is real and that you actually clean the kitchen after. I told her she was either lying or dating a different Yves Quinn.

What are you making? Give me one dish.

— Nat

Yves,

Miso short ribs at 11pm after a double shift — you're either a very tired person or a very dramatic one. Probably both, which is the family curse.

I'm glad the routine is real and not just something you invented to tell me. The "patient" thing — that's new, by the way. Good new. Keep it.

Papa sounded okay to me too when I called Sunday. Sometimes okay is enough for a week. We take it.

Get some sleep. And send Jess the leftovers — he's been texting me about you going dark on weeknights and I cannot be the middleman for two grown men who work in the same hotel.

Love you, idiot.
— Nat

Nat,

She's not lying, I clean the kitchen. It's part of it — leaving a mess would ruin the whole point. Last night was a braise. Short ribs, miso, a little ginger. Nothing complicated, just patient. Jess texted while it was in the oven and I basically ignored him for two hours, which he'll forgive because I told him there were leftovers.

Tabarnak, Nat, I'm tired in a different way lately. Not bad-tired. More like... weighted. The Hawthorne's been full, which is good, but the guests this week have been — anyway. The Averna is fine. One drink, then bed.

Papa called this afternoon. He sounded okay. Said the coffee was good. I didn't push.

— Y
